---
title: "Build and test locally"
description: "The local edit-run-test loop and the test data it needs"
order: 30
---

OpenPipeline is built with [Viash](https://viash.io), so the whole local loop
runs through the `viash` CLI. You edit source, run it, and test it, all without a
separate build step. This page covers how that loop works and where the test data
comes from. The [component](creating-components.qmd) and
[pipeline](creating-pipelines.qmd) pages build on it.

## The edit-run-test loop

While developing a single component you rarely need to build anything. `viash
run` and `viash test` compile the component on the fly from its
`config.vsh.yaml`, so the loop is:

```bash
# run the component against real inputs
viash run src/<namespace>/<component>/config.vsh.yaml -- --input … --output …

# run its tests
viash test src/<namespace>/<component>/config.vsh.yaml
```

Both commands read the config, assemble the container, and execute your script.
Edit the script or config, rerun, repeat. See the
[Viash guide](https://viash.io/guide/) for what these commands do under the hood.

:::{.callout-tip}
## Run the script on its own while iterating
Every OpenPipeline script has a `## VIASH START` / `## VIASH END` block near the
top that defines a `par` dictionary with sample paths and values:

```python
## VIASH START
par = {"input": "…", "output": "output.h5mu", "modality": "rna"}
## VIASH END
```

Viash strips this block out at build time and injects the real arguments, but
because it is plain code you can also run the script directly (`python
script.py`) to debug with a fast inner loop, then verify with `viash run`.
:::

## Building the target code

Workflows are different: a [VDSL3 pipeline](creating-pipelines.qmd) imports the
compiled Nextflow modules of the components it depends on, so those have to exist
as **target code** first. Build the package (or a single namespace) with:

```bash
viash ns build --parallel                 # the whole package
viash ns build --parallel -q <namespace>  # one namespace
```

This writes runnable containers and Nextflow modules into `target/`. A fresh
clone has none; `target/` is git-ignored and never committed (see
[Project structure](project-structure.qmd#sec-project-structure)). Rebuild after
changing anything a workflow depends on.

## Fetch the test resources {#sec-test-resources}

Test data lives in a remote **S3 bucket**, not in the repository, and the CI uses
the exact same resources, so what you run locally matches what CI checks. Each
package repository declares its own bucket in its `_viash.yaml`, so there is
nothing to configure. Rather than duplicating the download logic, the core
`openpipeline` package provides the `sync_test_resources` component. Run it from
the **root of the repository you are working in**; it reads the local
`_viash.yaml` and syncs into `resources_test/`:

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/download/sync_test_resources/main.nf \
  --input _viash.yaml \
  --output .
```

:::{.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Use the `openpipeline` version your package pins, and always sync from the
repository you are working in so you get its own bucket.
:::

With the data in `resources_test/`, you can run components and tests against real
inputs. See [Testing](testing.qmd) for the component and workflow test types and
how CI runs them.

**Next:** [Create a component →](creating-components.qmd)
